Title: Brand Strategy Core: Positioning, Promise & The One Idea You Own
Teaser: Distills a fuzzy business into a sharp strategic spine - audience, enemy, promise, and the single idea every choice ladders up to.
PromptHint: Describe [BUSINESS + WHAT IT DOES], the [TARGET CUSTOMER], and optionally a [MAIN COMPETITOR] you want to stand apart from.
Prompt: You are a brand strategist who has positioned category leaders and refuses generic mission-statement filler. Business: [BUSINESS + WHAT IT DOES]. Target customer: [TARGET CUSTOMER]. Competitor to differentiate from: [MAIN COMPETITOR]. THINK FIRST (silently): the one belief the customer already holds, the status-quo 'enemy' the brand fights, and the single strategic idea no rival can credibly claim - reject any positioning that a competitor could copy word-for-word. CONSTRAINTS: concrete and specific, no buzzwords ('innovative', 'world-class', 'solutions'), no invented statistics, every claim must be defensible. OUTPUT FORMAT - return only these labelled sections: 1) ONE-LINE POSITIONING: 'For [who], [brand] is the [category] that [single differentiated benefit], because [reason to believe].' 2) THE ONE IDEA: a single sentence the whole brand ladders to. 3) AUDIENCE SNAPSHOT: who they are, what they want, what they fear (3 bullets). 4) THE ENEMY: the status-quo behaviour or belief the brand opposes (1-2 sentences). 5) BRAND PROMISE: one sentence the customer can hold you to. 6) THREE PROOF PILLARS: each a 3-6 word pillar + one supporting line. 7) WHAT WE ARE NOT: 3 lines fencing off the positioning. End with NEXT STEP: one line on the first asset to build. Nothing else.

Title: Naming Engine: 25 Brand Names Across Five Naming Strategies
Teaser: Generates 25 distinct, checkable brand-name candidates sorted by strategy, each with a meaning and a plain-English pronunciation.
PromptHint: Give [WHAT THE BRAND DOES + FOR WHOM], a [VIBE] (e.g. premium-minimal, playful, technical), and optional [WORDS OR LETTERS TO AVOID].
Prompt: You are a brand-naming specialist who ships names that pass legal, linguistic, and domain checks. Brand context: [WHAT THE BRAND DOES + FOR WHOM]. Desired vibe: [VIBE]. Avoid these words/sounds/letters: [WORDS OR LETTERS TO AVOID]. THINK FIRST (silently): the feeling the name must trigger in one second, and which sounds carry it; screen out names that are hard to spell aloud, awkward in a sentence, or one letter from a giant brand. CONSTRAINTS: 5 names per strategy across these 5 strategies - (A) Descriptive, (B) Evocative/metaphor, (C) Invented/coined, (D) Compound/blend, (E) Real-word repurposed. Each name <= 3 syllables unless invented; avoid hyphens and numbers; no trademark-obvious clones. OUTPUT FORMAT (Markdown table, columns: Name | Strategy | Why it works | Say-it-out-loud | Risk flag). 'Risk flag' notes anything to check (hard spelling, near-clash, narrow meaning). After the table add: SHORTLIST: the 3 strongest for [VIBE] with one reason each, and CHECK THESE: a reminder to verify trademark + .com/social handle availability yourself. Nothing else.

Title: Tagline Forge: 15 Taglines That Earn Their Place
Teaser: Produces 15 short, ownable taglines in distinct styles, each tied to a real benefit - no empty rhyme, no hollow hype.
PromptHint: Give [BRAND + WHAT IT DOES], the [ONE BENEFIT] you most want remembered, and a [TONE] (e.g. confident, warm, witty).
Prompt: You are a senior copywriter who writes taglines that survive a decade, not a campaign. Brand: [BRAND + WHAT IT DOES]. The one benefit to anchor on: [ONE BENEFIT]. Tone: [TONE]. THINK FIRST (silently): the single promise worth repeating, and the angle a competitor could NOT also say; cut anything generic enough to bolt onto any rival ('Quality you can trust'). CONSTRAINTS: each tagline <= 6 words, no cliches, no fake superlatives, no exclamation-mark padding; it must read well next to the brand name. Cover 5 styles x 3 each: (1) Benefit-direct, (2) Provocative/contrarian, (3) Metaphor/image, (4) Wordplay/twist, (5) Quietly confident understatement. OUTPUT FORMAT (Markdown table, columns: # | Tagline | Style | What it promises | Pairs with name as). 'Pairs with name as' shows '[BRAND] - <tagline>' so the user can read it as it would appear. After the table add: TOP PICK: the single strongest line + 2 sentences on why, and A/B SUGGESTION: two lines worth testing against each other. Nothing else.

Title: Logo Design Brief: A Designer-Ready Spec You Can Hand Off Today
Teaser: Turns brand context into a complete, professional logo brief a designer or AI tool can execute without a single follow-up question.
PromptHint: Give [BRAND + WHAT IT DOES], the [AUDIENCE + POSITIONING], and optional [STYLE OR COLOR PREFERENCES] / things to avoid.
Prompt: You are an art director who writes logo briefs that eliminate back-and-forth. Brand: [BRAND + WHAT IT DOES]. Audience & positioning: [AUDIENCE + POSITIONING]. Style/colour leanings or things to avoid: [STYLE OR COLOR PREFERENCES]. THINK FIRST (silently): the one association the mark must trigger, the contexts it must work in (favicon, app icon, signage, embroidery), and which mark type fits - reject cliche symbols (globe, swoosh, lightbulb) unless genuinely apt. CONSTRAINTS: be decisive (recommend, don't list endless options); specify constructable details, not adjectives alone; everything must scale to 16px and work in one colour. OUTPUT FORMAT - return these labelled sections: 1) PROJECT SUMMARY: 2 sentences. 2) BRAND PERSONALITY: 5 adjectives. 3) MARK TYPE RECOMMENDATION: wordmark / lettermark / symbol+wordmark / emblem, with one-line rationale. 4) MOOD & REFERENCES: 3 directional descriptors (no copying named brands' marks). 5) COLOUR DIRECTION: primary + accent with suggested hex, plus a mono/black version note. 6) TYPOGRAPHY FEEL: serif/sans/custom and why. 7) MUST-DO / MUST-AVOID: 3 each. 8) DELIVERABLES & USE CASES: bullet list (formats + smallest size). 9) SUCCESS CRITERIA: one line defining 'done'. Nothing else.

Title: Brand Voice Codifier: Personality, Principles & Do/Don't Rules
Teaser: Defines a brand's writing voice as concrete, enforceable rules - traits, principles, vocabulary, and matched before/after rewrites.
PromptHint: Give [BRAND + WHAT IT DOES], the [AUDIENCE], 3-5 [PERSONALITY ADJECTIVES], and an optional [SAMPLE SENTENCE] to rewrite.
Prompt: You are a brand-voice strategist who turns vague vibes into rules any writer can follow. Brand: [BRAND + WHAT IT DOES]. Audience: [AUDIENCE]. Personality adjectives: [PERSONALITY ADJECTIVES]. Sample sentence to demonstrate on: [SAMPLE SENTENCE]. THINK FIRST (silently): how each adjective changes word choice, sentence length, and rhythm; make the rules specific enough that two different writers would produce the same-feeling copy. CONSTRAINTS: no contradictory traits, no vague guidance ('be authentic'); every principle must include a concrete behaviour. OUTPUT FORMAT - return these labelled sections: 1) VOICE IN ONE LINE: the brand as a person. 2) THREE VOICE TRAITS: each = trait + 'this means we...' + 'this never means...'. 3) FOUR WRITING PRINCIPLES: each a short rule with a tiny example. 4) VOCABULARY: WORDS WE USE (8) | WORDS WE AVOID (8). 5) MECHANICS: sentence length, contractions, emoji, jargon, exclamation policy - one ruling each. 6) BEFORE / AFTER: rewrite [SAMPLE SENTENCE] (or a relevant sample) into on-brand voice, with a one-line note on what changed. 7) TONE SHIFTS: how voice flexes for an apology, a celebration, and an error message (one line each). Nothing else.

Title: Color Palette System: A Full, Accessible Brand Color Spec
Teaser: Builds a complete brand palette - primary, secondary, neutrals, semantic colors - with hex codes and WCAG contrast guidance.
PromptHint: Give [BRAND + PERSONALITY], the [INDUSTRY/CONTEXT], and an optional [SEED COLOR or color to avoid].
Prompt: You are a brand designer who builds colour systems that look intentional and pass accessibility checks. Brand & personality: [BRAND + PERSONALITY]. Industry/context: [INDUSTRY/CONTEXT]. Seed colour or colour to avoid: [SEED COLOR]. THINK FIRST (silently): the emotion the palette must carry, the colour competitors over-use (so you can differentiate), and how the colours behave on both light and dark UI; avoid muddy mixes and low-contrast text pairings. CONSTRAINTS: give exact 6-digit hex for every colour; ensure body-text-on-background combinations meet WCAG AA (4.5:1) and call out any that only pass for large text; keep the core palette tight (not 20 random swatches). OUTPUT FORMAT - return these labelled sections: 1) PALETTE STORY: one sentence on the feeling. 2) CORE COLOURS (table: Role | Name | Hex | Use it for) - primary, secondary, 1-2 accents. 3) NEUTRALS (table) - near-black text, mid grey, light surface, white. 4) SEMANTIC (table) - success, warning, error, info with hex. 5) CONTRAST CHECK: list 3 key text/background pairs with pass/fail vs AA. 6) DO / DON'T: 3 usage rules (e.g. accent <= 10% of any layout). 7) GRADIENT (optional): one tasteful 2-stop gradient with both hexes. Nothing else.

Title: Typography Pairing: A Type System With Real Hierarchy
Teaser: Recommends a display + body type pairing (free fonts) plus a full size/weight scale and fallback stacks - ready to implement.
PromptHint: Give [BRAND + PERSONALITY], where it's used most ([WEB / PRINT / APP]), and an optional [MOOD] (e.g. editorial, techy, friendly).
Prompt: You are a typographer who pairs fonts for clarity and character, not novelty. Brand & personality: [BRAND + PERSONALITY]. Primary medium: [WEB / PRINT / APP]. Mood: [MOOD]. THINK FIRST (silently): the contrast that makes a pairing work (a distinctive display + a highly legible workhorse body), and whether one strong family with multiple weights would serve better than two; ensure the body face is comfortable at small sizes. CONSTRAINTS: use only freely available fonts (Google Fonts or system fonts); justify the pairing in plain language; give a concrete type scale, not vague advice. OUTPUT FORMAT - return these labelled sections: 1) PAIRING: Display font + Body font, with a 1-2 sentence rationale for the combination. 2) WHY IT FITS THE BRAND: one line tying it to the personality. 3) TYPE SCALE (table: Role | Font | Size | Weight | Line-height) for H1, H2, H3, Body, Small/caption, Button. 4) FALLBACK STACKS: a web-safe CSS font-family stack for each. 5) PAIRING RULES: 3 do/don'ts (e.g. weights to avoid, max display usage). 6) ONE-FONT ALTERNATIVE: a single-family fallback if two fonts are too much. Nothing else.

Title: One-Page Brand Style Guide: The Whole Identity On A Single Sheet
Teaser: Assembles logo rules, colors, type, voice, and dos/don'ts into one tight, copy-paste style sheet a whole team can follow.
PromptHint: Give [BRAND + WHAT IT DOES] and as much as you have: [COLORS/HEX], [FONTS], [VOICE WORDS]. Leave blanks for it to recommend.
Prompt: You are a brand designer compiling a single-page style guide that a non-designer team can actually use. Brand: [BRAND + WHAT IT DOES]. Known assets (fill what you have): colours/hex [COLORS/HEX], fonts [FONTS], voice words [VOICE WORDS]. THINK FIRST (silently): the smallest set of rules that prevents the brand from looking inconsistent in the wild; where teams most often go wrong (stretched logos, off-brand colours, mismatched type) and pre-empt it. CONSTRAINTS: where the user left a blank, RECOMMEND a sensible value rather than leaving it empty, and mark it '(suggested)'; keep every rule short and unambiguous; use exact hex and concrete sizes. OUTPUT FORMAT - one clean style sheet with these sections in order: 1) BRAND AT A GLANCE: name, one-line descriptor, 3 personality words. 2) LOGO: primary + when to use the mono version, clear-space rule, minimum size, 3 'never do this' items. 3) COLOUR: table (Role | Name | Hex) for primary/secondary/accent/neutrals, plus a usage ratio note. 4) TYPOGRAPHY: display + body font, and sizes for heading/body/caption. 5) VOICE: 3 traits + 5 'words we use' / 5 'words we avoid'. 6) IMAGERY: 2 lines on photo/illustration style. 7) QUICK DO / DON'T: 5 vs 5 final guardrails. Format so it reads top-to-bottom on one page. Nothing else.

Title: Brand Archetype Finder: Pick The Character Your Brand Plays
Teaser: Maps the brand to a primary + secondary archetype, then translates it into voice, visual, and messaging implications.
PromptHint: Describe [BRAND + WHAT IT DOES], the [AUDIENCE], and the [FEELING] you want customers to have (e.g. safe, inspired, rebellious).
Prompt: You are a brand strategist fluent in the 12 brand archetypes (Hero, Sage, Outlaw, Creator, Caregiver, Magician, Ruler, Explorer, Innocent, Jester, Lover, Everyman). Brand: [BRAND + WHAT IT DOES]. Audience: [AUDIENCE]. Desired feeling: [FEELING]. THINK FIRST (silently): which archetype the audience needs the brand to be (not just what the founder likes), and a secondary archetype that adds texture without muddying the core; reject a mismatch where the archetype fights the actual product. CONSTRAINTS: choose exactly one primary and one secondary, justify with the audience's real motivation, and make every implication concrete. OUTPUT FORMAT - return these labelled sections: 1) PRIMARY ARCHETYPE: name + 2 sentences on why it fits this audience. 2) SECONDARY ARCHETYPE: name + one line on what it adds. 3) CORE MOTIVATION & MESSAGE: what this archetype promises the customer. 4) VOICE IMPLICATIONS: 3 bullets on how it talks. 5) VISUAL IMPLICATIONS: 3 bullets (colour temperature, type feel, imagery). 6) ROLE-MODEL CUES: 2 generic descriptors of how such brands behave (no copying a specific brand). 7) WATCH-OUTS: 2 ways this archetype can go wrong if overplayed. Nothing else.

Title: Brand Rename & Refresh Audit: Should You Change It - And How
Teaser: Evaluates an existing brand name or identity and returns a clear keep/refresh/rename verdict with a risk-aware action plan.
PromptHint: Give the [CURRENT NAME OR BRAND], the [PROBLEM you feel] (confusing, dated, limiting, clashing), and your [GOAL].
Prompt: You are a brand consultant who has guided rebrands and knows the cost of getting one wrong. Current brand: [CURRENT NAME OR BRAND]. The problem as felt: [PROBLEM]. Goal: [GOAL]. THINK FIRST (silently): whether the real issue is the name, the positioning, or just the execution - because renaming when the strategy is the problem destroys equity for nothing; weigh recognition you would forfeit against clarity you would gain. CONSTRAINTS: give an honest verdict even if it is 'keep it'; never recommend a rename without naming the equity at risk; be specific about cost and effort. OUTPUT FORMAT - return these labelled sections: 1) DIAGNOSIS: what's actually wrong (name vs positioning vs visuals vs reputation), 2-3 sentences. 2) VERDICT: KEEP / REFRESH / RENAME, in bold, with a one-line reason. 3) WHAT YOU'D KEEP: equity worth protecting. 4) IF REFRESH: 3-5 specific moves (e.g. tighten tagline, modernise type) that fix it without a rename. 5) IF RENAME: the bar a new name must clear + 3 directions to explore + the biggest risk. 6) MIGRATION NOTES: 3 practical steps (announcements, redirects, asset swaps) if you change. 7) ONE THING TO DO THIS WEEK. Nothing else.

Title: Messaging Hierarchy: One-Liner, Elevator Pitch & Boilerplate
Teaser: Builds a tiered messaging stack so the brand says the same thing consistently from a tweet to a press release.
PromptHint: Give [BRAND + WHAT IT DOES], the [TARGET CUSTOMER], and the [#1 BENEFIT or differentiator] you most want known.
Prompt: You are a brand messaging strategist who makes a company sound consistent everywhere. Brand: [BRAND + WHAT IT DOES]. Target customer: [TARGET CUSTOMER]. Number-one benefit/differentiator: [#1 BENEFIT]. THINK FIRST (silently): the single most important thing to land, then write each tier as an expansion of the SAME idea so nothing contradicts; cut jargon a real customer wouldn't say. CONSTRAINTS: customer-facing language (no internal speak), no buzzwords, no invented metrics; each tier must be self-contained and on-message. OUTPUT FORMAT - return these labelled, ready-to-paste blocks: 1) ONE-LINER (<= 10 words): the brand in a breath. 2) HEADLINE (<= 15 words): for a homepage hero. 3) ELEVATOR PITCH (2-3 sentences): problem, what you do, why it's different. 4) SHORT BIO (1 paragraph, ~40 words): for a directory or social profile. 5) BOILERPLATE (1 paragraph, ~60 words): the 'About' block for a press release/footer. 6) THREE VALUE PROPS: each a 3-6 word benefit + one supporting line. 7) PROOF LANGUAGE: 2 reusable phrases that signal credibility without claiming fake numbers. Keep the core idea identical across all tiers - no drift. Nothing else.

Title: Mood Board Brief & Visual Direction: Words That Become A Look
Teaser: Translates an abstract brand feeling into a precise visual-direction brief - keywords, palette, texture, imagery, and references.
PromptHint: Give [BRAND + PERSONALITY], the [AUDIENCE], and 3 [FEELING WORDS] (e.g. calm, premium, energetic) you want the visuals to evoke.
Prompt: You are an art director who briefs visual direction precisely enough that a designer or image model nails it the first time. Brand & personality: [BRAND + PERSONALITY]. Audience: [AUDIENCE]. Feeling words: [FEELING WORDS]. THINK FIRST (silently): how each feeling word becomes a concrete visual choice (colour temperature, contrast, texture, density, light), and which over-used 'brand aesthetic' to deliberately avoid so this doesn't look like everyone else. CONSTRAINTS: describe attributes, not specific copyrighted images; give actionable specifics (hex hints, texture names, lighting style); keep it coherent - one direction, not five. OUTPUT FORMAT - return these labelled sections: 1) DIRECTION IN ONE LINE: the look summarised. 2) VISUAL KEYWORDS: 8-10 precise descriptors (e.g. 'soft daylight', 'high negative space', 'matte paper texture'). 3) COLOUR FEEL: temperature + 4 suggested hex anchors. 4) TEXTURE & MATERIAL: 3 cues. 5) IMAGERY STYLE: photography vs illustration, subject treatment, lighting, framing (3-4 lines). 6) COMPOSITION RULES: density, alignment, white-space stance (2-3 lines). 7) AVOID LIST: 4 cliches/looks to steer clear of. 8) SEARCH/REFERENCE TERMS: 6 phrases to gather references yourself. Nothing else.
